coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of ultrafine PtPd alloy/ionic liquid-carbon dots nanocomposites modified flexible carbon fiber as enzyme-free amperometric sensing for H2O2 in living cells

coredata.dc:description: Given the pivotal role of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in numerous biological progresses, there has been a recent surge in the development of highly effective electrochemical sensors for H2O2 detection. In this article, we propose an enzyme-free amperometric system for the sensitive and selective recognition of H2O2, leveraging ultrafine PtPd alloy/ionic liquid-carbon dots (IL-CDs) nanocomposites modified flexible carbon fiber. The IL-CDs were synthesized through the direct electrodeposition of ionic liquid [BMIM][PF6] using Pt sheets as electrodes. Subsequently, a sequential electrodeposition of IL-CDs, H2PtCl6, and PdCl2 onto the activated carbon fiber (ACF) yielded the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode. This microelectrode serves as an enzyme-free amperometric sensor for H2O2 detection, exhibiting an improved response with a significantly lower detection limit of 0.29 μM and a relatively broad linear range spanning from 2 to 6561 μM. Furthermore, this enzyme-free amperometric H2O2 sensor demonstrates remarkable stability over extended periods and exceptional performance in practical applications, such as the analysis of commercially available milk, human serum, human urine, and living cancer cells analysis. This study offers a fresh perspective on amperometric sensors based on metal alloys and CDs electrode, highlighting the potential of enzyme-independent detection for hydrogen peroxide in biological systems.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of CF (A), ACF (B), IL-CDs/ACF (C), PtPd/ACF (D), and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (E).

XPS survey spectra of IL-CDs and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (A), high-resolution Pt 4f XPS spectrum of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (B), high-resolution Pd 3d XPS spectrum of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (C), high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum of IL-CDs (D) and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (E).

CV curves (A) and EIS curves (B) of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− in 0.1 M KCl solution at bare ACF (a), IL-CDs/ACF (b), PdPt/ACF (c), and PdPt/IL-CDs/ACF (d). (C) CV curves of bare ACF (a), IL-CDs/ACF (b), Pd/IL-CDs/ACF (c), Pt/IL-CDs/ACF (d), and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (e) in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.

(A) CV curves of 10 mM H2O2 at bare ACF (a), IL-CDs/ACF (b), Pd/IL-CDs/ACF (c), Pt/IL-CDs/ACF (d), PtPd/ACF (e), and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (f) in nitrogen-saturated 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 10 mM H2O2. (B) CV curves of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF in nitrogen-saturated 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 0 (a), 5 (b), 10 (c), and 20 mM (d) H2O2. (C) CV curves of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF in nitrogen-saturated 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) at different scan rate of 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 240, 280, and 300 mV·s−1. (D) Amperometric i-t curves of ACF (a), IL-CDs/ACF (b), PtPd/ACF(c), and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (d) in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) after adding 100 µM H2O2 for three times.

Dependences of amperometric currents on the concentration of IL-CDs (A), the deposition potential of IL-CDs (B), the deposition time of IL-CDs (C), the concentration of PtPd (D), the deposition time of PtPd (E), the deposition potential of PtPd (F), the pH value of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (G), and the applied potential of H2O2 (H). The error bars in each condition represent the standard deviations of three separate measurements.

(A) Typical amperometric response of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode to successive addition of different H2O2 concentration (from 2 µM to 13 mM) in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). Inset: Amperometric response of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode at low concentrations of H2O2. Applied potential: − 0.1 V. (B) Relationship between the amperometric current and the concentration of H2O2 (from 2 µM to 13 mM). Inset: Relationship between the amperometric current and the concentration of H2O2 (from 2 µM to 6561 µM). The error bars in each condition represent the standard deviations of three separate measurements.

(A) Relative amperometric response of five PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrodes to successive addition of 100 mM H2O2 concentration in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). (B) Relative amperometric response of same PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrodes to successive addition of 100 mM H2O2 concentration in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) for different days. The error bars in each condition represent the standard deviations of three separate measurements.

(A) Amperometric response of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF to successive addition of 100 mM H2O2 and 100 mM AA, DA, UA, Glu, L-Cys, and Na2SO4 in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). Applied potential: − 0.1 V. (B) Amperometric responses of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 100 µM fMLP with or without A549 cells and/or 500 U·mL−1 of catalase. Curve a: without A549 cells; Curve b: with A549 cells and catalase; Curve c: with A549 cells. (C) Images of A549 cells before (Left) and after (Right) the addition of 100 μM fMLP.
The schematic illustration of the preparation and the application of such enzyme-free electrochemical smperometric biosensor for H2O2 detection.
Comparison of our amperometric biosensor and other electrochemical methods for H2O2 detection.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of ultrafine PtPd alloy/ionic liquid-carbon dots nanocomposites modified flexible carbon fiber as enzyme-free amperometric sensing for H2O2 in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the pivotal role of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in numerous biological progresses, there has been a recent surge in the development of highly effective electrochemical sensors for H2O2 detection. In this article, we propose an enzyme-free amperometric system for the sensitive and selective recognition of H2O2, leveraging ultrafine PtPd alloy/ionic liquid-carbon dots (IL-CDs) nanocomposites modified flexible carbon fiber. The IL-CDs were synthesized through the direct electrodeposition of ionic liquid [BMIM][PF6] using Pt sheets as electrodes. Subsequently, a sequential electrodeposition of IL-CDs, H2PtCl6, and PdCl2 onto the activated carbon fiber (ACF) yielded the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode. This microelectrode serves as an enzyme-free amperometric sensor for H2O2 detection, exhibiting an improved response with a significantly lower detection limit of 0.29 μM and a relatively broad linear range spanning from 2 to 6561 μM. Furthermore, this enzyme-free amperometric H2O2 sensor demonstrates remarkable stability over extended periods and exceptional performance in practical applications, such as the analysis of commercially available milk, human serum, human urine, and living cancer cells analysis. This study offers a fresh perspective on amperometric sensors based on metal alloys and CDs electrode, highlighting the potential of enzyme-independent detection for hydrogen peroxide in biological systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), a crucial component of reactive oxygen species, plays a pivotal role in diverse biological processes, including immune response and physiological signaling, resulting from its generation during enzyme-catalyzed reactions in humans [1]. Any imbalance in the production and consumption of H2O2 can potentially trigger oxidative stress, a phenomenon frequently associated with aging and various pathological conditions, such as cancer initiation, neurological disorders, and cardiovascular disease [2,3]. Reports indicate that the intracellular concentration of H2O2 ranges from 0.05 to 0.7 μM, while the extracellular concentration of H2O2 is approximately 10 times higher [4]. However, these concentrations can be locally enhanced under pathological conditions [4]. Therefore, accurately and efficiently detecting H2O2 in living cells is of remarkable importance for understanding its role in cellular physiology and pathology [5]. Until now, various techniques have been developed for H2O2 sensing, including colorimetry [6], photoluminescence [2,7], chemiluminescence [8], electrogenerated chemiluminescence [9], photoelectrochemical method [10], and electrochemical strategy [11]. Among these techniques, electrochemical sensing stands out due to its cost-effectiveness, ease of use, rapid response, exceptional sensitivity, and excellent selectivity [3,4]. In recent decades, electrochemical biosensors have undergone significant advancements, particularly in the detection of H2O2 [12]. While enzyme-based electrochemical biosensors offer remarkable selectivity, they are not without their limitations. These limitations include vulnerability to environmental conditions, high costs, and complex immobilization procedures. As a result, there has been a growing interest in the development of functional nanocomposite-based electrochemical biosensors that do not require enzymes or proteins.

Until now, various metal-based nanocomposites have been extensively explored, and remarkable progress has been made in utilizing carbon-supported nanocomposites for the efficient electrochemical detection of H2O2, demonstrating exceptional capabilities in this domain [13–15]. Bimetallic nanostructures, which harness the combined effects of two distinct metals, have gained significant attention for practical applications. Compared to their monometallic counterparts, these nanostructures offer superior characteristics, including enhanced catalytic performances, exceptional selectivity in catalysis, and improved resistance against deactivation [16,17]. Noble bimetallic PtPd alloys, known for their large surface areas and high activity in oxygen reduction reactions, have garnered considerable attention in the electrocatalytic sector due to their outstanding electrocatalytic capabilities among other bimetallic nanocomposites [18,19]. PtPd alloy nanoparticles have been reported to exhibit enhanced stability, superior electrochemical properties, and improved catalytic efficacy in glucose and ethanol oxidation, as well as in the reduction of H2O2 [20]. A novel class of carbon nanostructures, known as carbon dots (CDs), has garnered significant attention in recent years due to their remarkable qualities. These include chemical inertness, near-nontoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and ease of modification [21]. CDs possess an extensive array of functional groups present on their surface, making it easy for metal ions to assemble onto the surface of CDs through complexation reaction [22]. In recent years, ionic liquids (ILs) have garnered huge attention in both academia and industrial settings [23]. These neoteric materials are widely used as the precursors to prepare functional CDs [22,24]. Given the outstanding electrocatalytic properties of metal-based nanocomposites, combined with the excellent compatibility of IL-CDs with living organisms, the utilization of metal-infused nanocomposites and IL-CDs in electrochemical sensing approaches has the potential to significantly enhance detection capabilities for specific targets [25].

Extensive research has been conducted on the development and application of flexible electrode substrates. Carbon-based materials, particularly carbon fiber (CF), have gained widespread popularity in the construction of electrochemical biosensors due to their remarkable characteristics of high chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, rapid electron and mass transfer, inherent mechanical strength, and acceptable flexibility [26]. These modified electrodes demonstrate exceptional biological information capacity for both in vitro and in vivo identification. Motivated by these findings, we have innovated a flexible activated CF (ACF) microelectrode that is modified with ultrafine PtPd alloy and IL-CDs (Scheme 1 ). The IL molecules bridged onto the surface of IL-CDs provide sufficient active sites for the nucleation of bimetallic PtPd nanoparticles, significantly enhancing their nucleation rate. This enhancement facilitates the formation of high-density and ultrafine bimetallic PtPd nanoparticles on the IL-CDs/ACF components [27]. By combining the structural advantages and electrocatalytic properties of PtPd and IL-CDs within the ACF framework, we have established a highly sensitive, discriminative, and reliable amperometric biosensing platform for the electrochemical enzyme-free analysis of H2O2. This electrochemical biosensor has been successfully employed for the detection of H2O2 in milk and bodily fluids, as well as for real-time tracking of H2O2 secretion from human lung cancer cell lines A549. Such amperometric biosensor boasts remarkable durability, user-friendliness, high specific activity, and excellent electron transfer capabilities during the direct redox action of H2O2 on modified electrode surface, which holds significant implications in the design and exploration of effective strategies for clinical diagnosis and treatment of cancer diseases.

1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([BMIM][PF6]), H2PtCl6·6H2O, PdCl2, acetonitrile, and various other chemicals were sourced from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. N-Formylmethionyl-leucyl-phenylalanine (fMLP) and catalase were purchased from Shanghai Sigma-Aldrich Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. Millipore-Q Academic purification set was utilized to provide ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ∙cm).

All electrochemical measurements were performed using Shanghai Chenhua CHI-760E electrochemical workstation by using platinum wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were captured using a Hitachi S-3400 N Scanning Electron Microscopy. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired on a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi instrument. All pH measurements were carried out utilizing a Sartorius PB-10 basic pH meter.

IL-CDs were prepared according to the reported method [24,28]. Briefly, two parallel Pt sheets were arranged side by side into the mixture of [BMIM][PF6]/acetonitrile (volume ratio of 1:500) with a measure of separation 3.0 cm. The electrodes were subjected to a static potential of 15 V for some time until the color of electrolyte turned from colorless to dark brown. Ultimately, the cathode underwent precipitation and concurrently induced a shift in solution color to yellow. The solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm, and the supernatant was dialyzed using a dialysis bag with 500 Da MWCO. The IL-CDs were solidified through a 48-hour vacuum freeze-drying process and subsequently stored at a temperature of 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and application of amperometric biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a collection of CFs was washed ultrasonically by C3H6O, C2H5OH, and ultrapure water for several times. Following that, CFs were engrossed in the mixture of H2O2 and C2H5OH (volume ratio of 1:1) at 60 °C for 48 h to obtain ACF. IL-CDs modified ACF (IL-CDs/ACF) was obtained via the electrodeposition of IL-CDs on ACF in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte containing 2 mg∙mL−1 IL-CDs with constant potential of − 2.0 V for 10 min by using ACF as the work electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and Pt wire as the counter electrode. The PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF was prepared by further electrodeposition of H2PtCl6 and PdCl2 on IL-CDs/ACF in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution containing 3.0 mM H2PtCl6 and 3.0 mM PdCl2 with constant potential of − 0.2 V for 300 s. For comparison, Pt/IL-CDs/ACF, Pd/IL-CDs/ACF, and PtPd/ACF underwent identical procedural measures during preparation. All microelectrodes were rinsed using ultrapure water for several times and freeze-dried before detection.
The detection of H2O2 was conducted using the amperometric method under room temperature in 10 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) at constant stirring and fixed detection potential of − 0.1 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Treatment of authentic samples and cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Milk was purchased from the local market. A 2 mL milk sample was placed in a centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of 4 mL of trichloroacetic acid and 2.0 mL of chloroform to precipitate organic matter. Subsequently, the precipitate was removed through centrifugation at 8,000 g for 5 min. The supernatant was then dissolved in 10 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), and various concentrations of H2O2 were introduced to the sample for conducting a standard addition recovery experiment.

All serum and urine samples were obtained from a cohort of individuals who exhibited optimal health, within the confines of our laboratory. The human serum and urine samples underwent centrifugation at 10,000 g for 5 min, after which they were filtered using a membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm. The pretreated samples were stored at 4 ℃. Prior to the measurement, the pretreated human serum and urine samples were supplemented with H2O2 solutions of varying concentrations as per the standard protocol. After a thorough mixing, the samples underwent a 50-fold dilution using 10 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The concentration of H2O2 was detected via the standard addition recovery method.

The human lung adenocarcinoma A549 cells were cultured in an appropriate medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin (10,000 IU penicillin and 10,000 μg∙mL−1 streptomycin). The cell culture was maintained at 37 °C under humidified conditions with 5 % CO2 in a humid atmosphere. The media was Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Multicell) for every single living cell.

To investigate the morphology of different electrochemical sensing platforms, SEM was employed to characterize CFs with various treatments and modifications. Fig. 1 A displays the SEM image of pristine CF, revealing its distinct characteristics as a highly pliable and resilient material, ideally suited as a smooth substrate for flexible microelectrodes. The CF, typically measuring diameter of approximately 10 μm, exhibit exceptional properties that underscore its potential as an outstanding foundation for various microelectrode applications. For the fabrication of IL-CDs-modified CF, the chemical activation of the CF was carried out to enhance their surface roughness and hydrophilic properties (Fig. 1B), which facilitated the subsequent electrodeposition of IL-CDs onto their surface. Subsequently, the ACF served as the working electrode in a Na2SO4 electrolyte containing IL-CDs, and the electrodeposition of IL-CDs was carried out at an exceptionally low potential of − 2.0 V. Throughout this procedure, the ACF was uniformly coated with IL-CDs nanoparticles (Fig. 1C). Apart from enhancing the ACF's electrocatalytic activity, the IL-CD modification increased its active surface area and provided additional active sites for the nucleation of metal nanoparticles. To fabricate the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode, the electrodeposition technique was employed to deposit Pt and Pd onto the IL-CDs/ACF surface, resulting in an ultrahigh density and uniform particle size distribution of PtPd alloy (Fig. 1E). In contrast, when PtPd was deposited directly onto untreated ACF, the dispersion was relatively large and uneven (Fig. 1D). The [BMIM][PF6] molecules, which benefit from the abundant active sites provided by the IL-CDs, facilitated PtPd nucleation and increased its nucleation rate due to the reduced interfacial tension of IL [29]. These factors contributed to the formation of IL-CDs/ACF with a high concentration of densely packed PtPd nanoparticles. Such a microelectrode surface, composed of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF, is particularly superior in terms of its sensitive catalytic efficacy for the reduction of H2O2.

To further elucidate the formation of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF, XPS analysis was conducted to examine the elemental composition and investigate the electronic interactions occurring on the surface of ACF. The XPS survey spectra of IL-CDs and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF are depicted in Fig. 2 A. The XPS survey spectrum of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF reveals six distinct peaks, corresponding to Pt 4f, Pd 3d, C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and F 1 s. The high-resolution Pt 4f XPS spectrum of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (Fig. 2B) shows a doublet peak located at the binding energies of 71.6 and 74.9 eV, which can be assigned to the Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2, respectively. Similarly, the high-resolution Pd 3d XPS spectrum of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF (Fig. 2C) displays the peaks at the binding energies of 335.8 and 341.1 eV, which are attributed to the Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2, respectively [30]. Furthermore, the high-resolution C 1 s XPS data for IL-CDs shows four distinct peaks, each associated with binding energies at 284.70, 285.85, 287.15, and 288.30 eV, which can be ascribed to the C = C, C − O, C = O, and O − C = O, respectively [31]. Following the electrochemical deposition of IL-CDs onto the ACF surface, the C 1 s XPS spectrum of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF exhibits a similar composition with four distinct peaks at 284.85 eV (C = C), 285.64 eV (C − O), 286.82 eV (C = O), and 288.45 eV (O − C = O), respectively [32,33]. The similarity in peak patterns between the IL-CDs and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF samples indicates that the IL-CDs and PtPd nanomaterial have been successfully modified onto the ACF surface using the one-step electrochemical deposition technique. This modification not only enhances the surface area and active sites of the ACF but also facilitates the nucleation and growth of PtPd nanoparticles, resulting in a superior electrochemical biosensor with enhanced sensitivity for H2O2 reduction.

The typical cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− in 0.1 M KCl solution have been acquired for bare ACF, IL-CDs/ACF, PtPd/ACF, and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the CV profiles of all microelectrodes exhibit a distinct redox peak pair, aligning with the quasi-reversible one-electron redox potentials of [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− on the surfaces of these microelectrodes. Notably, the peak currents observed at both the anode and cathode are increased significantly upon the deposition of IL-CDs onto the ACF surface. This augmentation indicates that IL-CDs enhance the electron transfer kinetics of [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−, which might be attributed to the excellent conductivity of IL-CDs [34]. Meanwhile, the anodic and cathodic peak currents are also elevated upon the deposition of PtPd onto the ACF exterior. Remarkably, the charging current and current limitation of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF are substantially greater than those of ACF, IL-CDs/ACF, and PtPd/ACF, suggesting that the utilization of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF effectively enhances the electron mass transfer rate through its increased active surface area and improved electrical conductivity. Moreover, to highlight the distinct electrochemical characteristics of the various microelectrodes, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) values of bare ACF, IL-CDs/ACF, PtPd/ACF, and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF were determined using the [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− redox couple as an electrochemical probe. According to the Randles-Sevcik equation [35], the calculated ECSA values of bare ACF, IL-CDs/ACF, PtPd/ACF, and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF are 0.18 cm2, 0.28 cm2, 0.42 cm2, and 0.55 cm2, respectively. Notably, the significantly larger active surface area of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF is advantageous for enhanced electron transfer efficiency on electrode surface.

The electrochemical properties of electrode materials were thoroughly investigated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 3B, the semicircular portion of the EIS curve represents the electron transfer limitation process, with its diameter serving as an indicator of the electron transfer resistance (R et) encountered by the electrochemically active species on the microelectrode surface. A reduction in diameter signifies a decrease in the R et value, indicating improved electron transfer efficiency. For the ACF microelectrode, the electron transfer was observed to be relatively sluggish (curve a in Fig. 3B), leading to a relatively high R et value of 3.35 Ω. However, upon the deposition of IL-CDs on the ACF surface, the semicircle diameter diminished (curve b in Fig. 3B), translating to a decreased R et value of 2.65 Ω. This decrease highlights the excellent conductivity of IL-CDs, which facilitated a faster rate of electron transfer. Furthermore, following the deposition of PtPd nanoparticles onto the ACF surface, the R et value was further reduced to 2.44 Ω (curve c in Fig. 3B). This reduction can be attributed to the efficient electron transfer promoted by the presence of bimetallic PtPd nanoparticles. Remarkably, the R et value of the PdPt/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode was significantly lower than that the aforementioned microelectrodes (curve d in Fig. 3B), with a R et value of merely 1.49 Ω. This observation underscores the larger effective area and enhanced conductivity of the PdPt/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode, which collectively contribute to a substantially improved electron transfer rate. The consistency between the EIS and CV results provides compelling evidence of the successful modification of the ACF surface with IL-CDs and PtPd alloy, further validating the enhanced electrochemical performance of the composite microelectrode.

Furthermore, the electrochemical activities of bare ACF, IL-CDs/ACF, Pd/IL-CDs/ACF, Pt/IL-CDs/ACF, and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF were evaluated using CV measurements in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. As depicted in Fig. 3C, due to the absence of metal deposition at the surface of electrode, no redox peaks are observed on the bare ACF and IL-CDs/ACF surfaces. However, distinct redox peaks corresponding to the reductions of Pd and Pt are evident for Pd/IL-CDs/ACF and Pt/IL-CDs/ACF, respectively, occurring at approximately + 0.4 V. Notably, the presence of similar but broadened peaks for PdPt/IL-CDs/ACF verifies the successful co-deposition of PtPd onto the IL-CDs/ACF surface [36], indicating the formation of a PtPd alloy on the surface of microelectrode.

CV is also used for the evaluation of the modified electrodes' electrochemical characteristics. Fig. 4 A presents the CV curves of bare ACF, IL-CDs/ACF, Pd/IL-CDs/ACF, Pt/IL-CDs/ACF, PtPd/ACF, and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF in a nitrogen-saturated 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 10 mM H2O2. As depicted in Fig. 4A, neither the bare ACF nor the IL-CDs/ACF exhibited any reduction peaks for H2O2. In contrast, the reduction peak currents for H2O2 on Pd/IL-CDs/ACF and Pt/IL-CDs/ACF were significantly higher than those on ACF and IL-CDs/ACF. This increase in electrocatalytic properties of the working electrode can be attributed to the presence of Pd and Pt nanoparticles. Notably, the reduction peak of H2O2 was evident at − 0.23 V for PtPd/ACF, indicating its superior electrochemical activity for the reduction of H2O2 compared to both Pd/IL-CDs/ACF and Pt/IL-CDs/ACF. Furthermore, it is clearly visible that the electrochemical reduction peak current of H2O2 at − 0.23 V was further enhanced on PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF, being considerably higher than that on PdPt/ACF. This demonstrates the remarkable synergistic effect achieved by combining PtPd nanoparticles with IL-CDs [35]. The collaborative outcome underscores the improved performance of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF for electrochemcial applications, especially those involving the reduction of H2O2.

Fig. 4B provides further insights into the elctrocatalytic properties of the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode towards the reduction of H2O2. As the concentration of H2O2 increases from 0 to 20 mM, a corresponding elevation in the reduction peak current is observed. This clearly demonstrates that the PtPd/IL-CDs catalysts exhibit a strong and sensitive response toward the reduction of H2O2. Fig. 4C demonstrates the CV curves of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF in nitrogen-saturated 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) at various scan rate (v) ranging from 60 mV·s−1 to 300 mV·s−1. It is apparent that the peak current exhibits an upward trend as the scan rate increases. The redox peak current exhibits a linear correlation with the scan rate, and the linear equation is described as I(μA) = − 1294.11v(V·s−1) + 97.74 (correlation coefficient of 0.9910). This linear relationship indicates that the electrode reaction is controlled by the surface, suggesting efficient electron transfer and the dominance of surface-active sites during the electrocatalytic process [36]. This characteristic makes PtPd/Il-CDs/ACF a promising candidate for electrochemical applications where surface-controlled reactions are crucial.

To showcase the exceptional electrocatalytic abilities of our PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF approach, we conducted a comparison of the amperometric reactions among four distinct electrodes. This comparison was carried out by sequentially injecting 100 µM of H2O2 into nitrogen-saturated 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) maintained at a potential of − 0.1 V. Fig. 4D clearly illustrates the stepwise current responses of ACF, IL-CDs/ACF, PtPd/ACF, and PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF upon exposure to H2O2. Among these, our PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode exhibits the most sensitive amperometric reaction, outperforming ACF, IL-CDs/ACF, and PtPd/ACF. The aforementioned findings collectively validate the superior electrocatalytic efficiency of PtPd bimetallic material in the H2O2 reduction process. This superiority can be elucidated from two main factors: firstly, a bimetallic catalyst with a larger surface area can potentially provide more catalytic active sites than monometallic catalysts; secondly, the electrocatalytic activity of alloys is greatly amplified through the effective exploitation of metal interactions [37].

To enhance the responsiveness of this enzyme-free amperometric biosensor for detecting H2O2, several experimental parameters require optimization. We use the amperometric current of 100 µM H2O2 into nitrogen-saturated 0.1 M phosphate buffer as a benchmark for this optimization process. The factors we need to fine-tune include the IL-CDs' concentration, deposition potential and deposition time of IL-CDs, concentration of PtPd, deposition potential and deposition time of PtPd, the pH value of phosphate buffer, and the detection potential of H2O2. By carefully adjusting these variables, we aim to achieve the highest sensitivity and accuracy in H2O2 detecting.

The concentration of IL-CDs on electrode surface plays a crucial role in the electrochemical reduction of H2O2. As evident from Fig. 5 A, the current attains its peak when the IL-CDs concentration increases from 1 mg·mL−1 to 2 mg·mL−1. This implies that at this specific concentration, the active sites on ACF are optimally utilized, thereby enhancing the electrochemical reactions involving H2O2 reduction. However, as the IL-CDs concentration continues to rise from 2 mg·mL−1 to 5 mg·mL−1, there is a noticeable decrease in the current. This decline can be attributed to potential blockage of the electrode surface or the formation of an excessively thick IL-CDs layer due to higher concentrations, which hinder electron transfer and subsequently diminish the efficiency of the electrochemical reduction of H2O2. Therefore, the optimal concentration of IL-CDs is determined to be 2 mg·mL−1. Furthermore, both the deposition potential and deposition time of IL-CDs are critical for the accurate H2O2 detection. The curves in Fig. 5B and 5C clearly demonstrate that the current attains its maximum when the deposition potential of IL-CDs is set at − 2.0 V and the deposition time of IL-CDs is 10 min. At a deposition potential of − 2.0 V, IL-CDs effectively deposit on the electrode surface, fostering the creation of a favorable electrochemical interface that facilitates the electrochemical reduction of H2O2. Additionally, a deposition time of 10 min allows for an even and moderate deposition of IL-CDs on the electrode surface, avoiding both excessive thickness and thinness, which ultimately optimizes the electrode's performance. In light of these findings, the recommended conditions for depositing IL-CDs on the microelectrode are as follows: a concentration of 2 mg·mL−1, a deposition potential of − 2.0 V, and a deposition time of 10 min.

The significance of PtPd concentration on the ACF surface is paramount in the electrochemical detection of H2O2. As demonstrated in Fig. 5D, the current increases as the PtPd concentration rises from 0.5 mM to 3 mM, with a slight decrease observed when the concentration exceeds 3 mM. This trend suggests that, within a specific range, enhancing the PtPd concentration boosts the electrode's electrochemical responsiveness of H2O2. However, an excessively thick catalyst layer, formed when the PtPd concentration surpasses 3 mM, might impede electron transfer, thereby compromising electrochemical performance. Hence, 3 mM PtPd is deemed the optimal concentration for maximizing the electrode's sensitivity to H2O2. The deposition potential and deposition time of PtPd are also vital for the detection of H2O2. Fig. 5E reveals that the highest current is attained at a deposition potential of − 0.2 V for PtPd, indicating the most effective deposition conditions. Variations in deposition potential from 0 to − 0.4 V significantly impact the current, with − 0.2 V yielding the most favorable results. This suggests that at this potential, PtPd deposition is most efficient, leading to the formation of high-density and ultrafine bimetallic PtPd nanoparticles on the IL-CDs/ACF and subsequently enhancing the electrochemical response of this microelectrode to H2O2. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 5F, the current progressively increases as the deposition time of PtPd extends from 50 to 300 s, stabilizing thereafter. This trend implies that the PtPd catalyst layer on the microelectrode surface reaches an optimal state at 300 s, resulting in the strongest response to H2O2. Extending the deposition time beyond this point does not significantly improve electrochemical performance. In conclusion, by carefully adjusting the PtPd concentration, deposition potential, and deposition time, the electrochemical responsiveness of the electrode to H2O2 can be optimized. The optimal conditions determined in this experiment are a PtPd concentration of 3 mM, a deposition potential of − 0.2 V, and a deposition time of 300 s. These parameters, when combined, yield the highest current, thereby enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of H2O2 detection.

The analysis of H2O2 can be influenced significantly by the pH value of 0.1 M phosphate buffer. As demonstrated in Fig. 5G, the current shows an increase as the pH value of 0.1 M phosphate buffer is raised from 5.0 to 7.0, indicating an enhancement in H2O2 detection sensitivity. However, further alkalizing the buffer to a pH 9.0 leads to a notable decrease in current, showing that an overly alkaline environment compromises detection accuracy. Considering the instability of H2O2 in strong acid and strong alkaline solutions and the observed changes in current, we determined that a pH of 7.0 provided the optimal conditions for sensitive and accurate detection of H2O2. At this pH, the buffer solution facilitated the most efficient electrochemical reactions, leading to the most reliable results, which is crucial in various biochemical and environmental applications. Detection potential, referring to the voltage applied during the electrochemical detection process, affects the sensitivity and selectivity of the measurement. As exhibited in Fig. 5H, the maximum current is observed at an applied potential of − 0.1 V. This specific potential is chosen for the detection of H2O2 because it maximizes the electrochemical response, leading to more accurate and reliable measurements. Performing the H2O2 reduction at − 0.1 V can reduces energy consumption and improves energy efficiency. Furthermore, there may be undesirable side reactions such as electrode oxidation or electrolyte decomposition at higher potentials. Selecting a lower potential helps reduce the occurrence of these side reactions, thus enhancing the accuracy and reliability of the experiment. Thus, the lower overpotential associated with the reduction of H2O2 on the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode minimizes the likelihood of significant disturbances in the current response of H2O2 caused by the introduction of endogenous electroactive substances commonly present in biological systems [27]. Through careful pH balancing of the buffer solution and optimization of the detection potential, we have attained remarkable levels of sensitivity and selectivity in the electrochemical detection of H2O2. This refined methodology not only enhances the precision of our measurements but also opens up new possibilities for more sophisticated biochemical and environmental applications that depend on accurate H2O2 detection.

Under these optimal detection parameters, an novel amperometric biosensor is employed in the precise identification of H2O2. Fig. 6 A illustrates the amperometric curve of the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode following the introduction of H2O2 across a range of concentrations. As observed in Fig. 6A, the amperometric current exhibits a gradual increase as the concentration of H2O2 increases from 2 µM to 13 mM. Notably, as depicted in Fig. 6B, the amperometric current is increased linearly when the concentration of H2O2 is increased from 2 to 6561 µM. Especially, the amperometric current exhibits good linear correlation with the concentration of H2O2 in this range, adhering to the linear equation I(μA) = 0.059c(µM) + 7.707 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9960. Ultimately, this biosensor achieves a remarkably low detection limit of 0.29 µM for H2O2 (S/N = 3). In comparison to other electrochemical methods for H2O2 analysis, as outlined in Table 1 , our amperometric biosensor offers a broader linear range and a significantly lower detection limit. The PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode combines the excellent catalytic properties of noble bimetallic PtPd nanoparticles, the high ionic conductivity of IL, and the high specific surface area and good electrical conductivity of ACF, which provides higher sensitivity and lower detection limits for enzyme-free H2O2 detection. Compared to reported CV method, the use of fixed potential amperometry may enable this work to achieve higher sensitivity and lower detection limits, particularly for low-concentration H2O2 detection.

The repeatability of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF is a critical parameter to evaluate its suitability for practical application. As demonstrated in Fig. 7 A, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of five modified microelectrodes for the detection of 100 mM H2O2 in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) was found to be only 5.5 %, suggesting that the repeatability of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode is remarkably high. In addition, the storage stability of PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF was evaluated and highlighted by a remarkable current retention rate of 97.5 % after being stored for 30 days (Fig. 7B). These findings clearly indicate that the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF displays exceptional catalytic efficiency, consistent durability, and excellent stability in the detection of H2O2, making it a highly promising candidate for practical sensing applications.

The practical application of a sensor heavily depends on its selectivity, a crucial parameter to consider. The ability to perceive of the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode towards H2O2 detection may be affected by the endogenous electroactive substances. To study the selectivity of the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode for H2O2 identification, the experiment commenced by introducing 100 mM H2O2 for the amperometric analysis followed by successive additions of various interfering substances: 100 mM ascorbic acid (AA), 100 mM dopamine (DA), 100 mM uric acid (UA), 100 mM glucose (Glu), 100 mM L-cysteine (L-Cys), and 100 mM Na2SO4. As indicated in Fig. 8 A, the current response remains almost constant upon the introduction of these interferents with same concentrations into 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), demonstrating the excellent selectivity of such PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode for H2O2 detection in biological matrix. The superior performance observed in the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode for H2O2 reduction can be attributed to its remarkably lower overpotential. This lower overpotential ensures minimal interference from endogenous electroactive biomolecules or proteins [27,46–49], which are often present in complex biological or chemical environments. By reducing the overpotential required for the reduction of H2O2, the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode is able to achieve higher sensitivity and accuracy in detecting and quantifying H2O2, while minimizing the impact of unwanted electroactive species that could otherwise interfere with the measurement.

To achieve real-time monitoring of H2O2 released from human lung adenocarcinoma A549 cells, the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode was utilized as the working electrode and precisely positioned close to the A549 cells using a micromanipulator within the test solution. This setup allows for continuous and direct detection of the H2O2 production from the A549 cells, offering valuable insights into their metabolic activities and potential pathological processes. As a stimulant, fMLP was employed to trigger the production of H2O2 in viable A549 cancer cells [27,50]. As demonstrated in Fig. 8B, when the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode was immersed in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 100 μM fMLP but devoid of A549 cells, no immediate reaction was observed (curve a in Fig. 8B), indicating the absence of H2O2. Conversely, upon the addition of 100 μM fMLP into the A549 cells culture medium, a significant amperometric response was evident in the PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode (curve c in Fig. 8B), suggesting the generation of H2O2 from these living cancer cells. Additionally, the cellular alterations such as cell retraction and disruption of the microfilament network, indicative of apoptosis, were observed in A549 cells treated with fMLP, potentially attributed to the cellular damage caused by the H2O2 produced by the A549 cells (Fig. 8C). Furthermore, when both 100 μM fMLP and 500 U·mL−1 catalase were introduced into the A549 cells culture medium, no current response was recorded, owing to the catalase-mediated metabolism of H2O2 into water and oxygen (curve b in Fig. 8B). These findings indicate that the observed changes are solely dependent on the H2O2 released by the viable cancer cells, thus continuously indicating that our PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode can be utilized as a highly sensitive, superior selective, and enzyme-free amperometric biosensor for the analysis of H2O2 in sophisticated biological environments. This application demonstrates the remarkable capabilities of our PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode in detecting trace concentration of H2O2, highlighting its potential in biomedical research and clinical diagnostics.

To validate the practical application of our PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode, the concentration of H2O2 was detected in authentic samples, including commercially available milk, human serum, and human urine. Prior to analysis, the samples were diluted for 50 times. During the detection process, the results were recorded after introducing a known concentration of H2O2 into the samples, which were spiked with 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) at an applied potential of − 0.1 V. The concentration of H2O2 was calculated according to the linear equation of I(μA) = 0.059c(µM) + 7.707. As presented in Table 2 , the recovery values of H2O2 are discovered in the ranges of 94.7 % to 103.9 %, demonstrating the excellent performance of our PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode for the practical H2O2 detection in real samples.

To validate the accuracy of our enzyme-free amperometric biosensor, we conducted an analysis of the concentration of added H2O2 in these authentic samples using the widely-accepted standard redox method (GB 1616–2003 and GB 5009.226–2016 in P. R. China). By using the Student’s t-test, we found minimal differences between the results obtained from these standard methods and our biosensor's readings (Table 2). This comparison underscores the remarkable accuracy and dependability of our PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode for practical H2O2 detection in real samples. Furthermore, it offers an efficient approach for product quality monitoring and food safety assessment, highlighting its potential for widespread application in these areas.

In conclusion, this study has successfully developed an enzyme-free amperometric biosensor for the highly sensitive detection of H2O2, leveraging a PtPd/IL-CDs/ACF microelectrode with a higher amperometric current and a lower potential for H2O2 reduction. Our amperometric biosensor demonstrates a remarkable limit of detection for H2O2 at 0.29 μM, with a linearity range extending from 2 to 6561 μM. Owing to its easy preparation, excellent electrocatalytic activity, high sensitivity, and durability, such biosensor detected H2O2 in real samples and living cancer cells successfully. Looking ahead, further research will explore the potential applications of this biosensor in clinical diagnostics, food safety monitoring, and environmental analysis. We aim to refine the sensor's performance, investigate its selectivity towards other analytes, and assess its long-term stability under various conditions. The ultimate goal is to develop a robust, cost-effective, and user-friendly biosensor that can be widely adopted in practical settings.
Declarations.

The authors declare that they have no conflict of interests. The authors declare that informed consents were obtained from human participants for any experimentation with human subjects. All human serum and human urine experiments were performed in accordance with the Guidelines of China (WS/T 225-2002), and approved by the ethics committee at Nanning Normal University.
